text: Text classification serves as a cornerstone for numerous natural language processing applications. While traditional classifiers heavily depend on manual feature engineering—such as human-defined dictionaries, knowledge bases, and complex tree kernels—these methods often struggle to capture deep semantic nuances and suffer from data sparsity. In this paper, we propose a recurrent convolutional neural network (RCNN) designed to learn text representations without the need for handcrafted features. Our architecture utilizes a bi-directional recurrent structure to capture broad contextual information, which introduces significantly less noise than traditional window-based neural networks. This is followed by a max-pooling layer that automatically identifies and extracts the most influential words for classification. We evaluated our model across four diverse datasets, covering topic, sentiment, and writing style classification in both English and Chinese. The experimental results demonstrate that the RCNN consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods, showing particular strength in handling document-level text.
